title: Unit Final: Reflection, Presentation, and Professional Growth

questions.prompt: What is the main purpose of reflection in professional growth?

choices.text: To review progress, recognize strengths, and identify next steps
choices.feedback: Correct. Reflection supports professional growth by helping students evaluate progress and plan future improvement.

choices.text: To avoid thinking about completed work
choices.feedback: Reflection requires looking back at work and progress, not avoiding it.

choices.text: To focus only on mistakes and ignore growth
choices.feedback: Strong reflection includes strengths, progress, and areas for improvement.

choices.text: To remove the need for revision
choices.feedback: Reflection can support revision, but it does not replace it.

questions.prompt: Which action is the best example of revision?

choices.text: Leaving a draft unchanged after feedback
choices.feedback: Revision involves using review or feedback to improve the work.

choices.text: Improving labels, organization, and explanations after review
choices.feedback: Correct. Revision means making work clearer and stronger after evaluating it.

choices.text: Submitting a first draft because revision takes too much effort
choices.feedback: Revision is part of strengthening quality and professionalism.

choices.text: Removing useful evidence so the work is shorter
choices.feedback: Revision should improve quality, not weaken the final product.

questions.prompt: Good presentation skills include clear delivery, professional tone, and organized ideas.

choices.text: True
choices.feedback: Correct. Strong presentation skills help an audience understand ideas clearly and professionally.

choices.text: False
choices.feedback: Presentation skills are stronger when ideas are organized and delivered clearly.

questions.prompt: A student is preparing to present a career portfolio. Which plan is strongest?

choices.text: Show random artifacts with no explanation
choices.feedback: A strong portfolio presentation should be organized and should explain each artifact clearly.

choices.text: Introduce the portfolio, explain selected artifacts, and close with a summary of growth
choices.feedback: Correct. This plan gives the presentation a clear beginning, middle, and end.

choices.text: Skip the introduction and read every label word for word
choices.feedback: A strong presentation should be explained, not only read.

choices.text: Use every portfolio item even if it weakens the focus
choices.feedback: A strong presentation should be selective and purposeful.

questions.prompt: Why is audience awareness important during a portfolio presentation?

choices.text: Because it helps the speaker decide what to explain and how to present it
choices.feedback: This is a strong idea, but the correct answer in this set gives the fuller reason about clarity and audience needs.

choices.text: Because the audience should guess what each artifact means
choices.feedback: The speaker should help the audience understand the artifacts.

choices.text: Because audience needs help determine what evidence and explanations will be most useful
choices.feedback: Correct. Audience awareness helps the speaker choose and explain content more effectively.

choices.text: Because the audience does not need a clear purpose
choices.feedback: A clear purpose matters even more when presenting to an audience.

questions.prompt: A student says, "My portfolio is stronger now because I added clearer labels, revised my explanations, and practiced speaking about my work." What does this best show?

choices.text: The student used reflection, revision, and presentation preparation together
choices.feedback: This is close, but the correct answer in this set is the fuller explanation of how multiple Unit 6 skills are being combined.

choices.text: The student should avoid presenting the portfolio
choices.feedback: The statement shows preparation for presenting, not avoidance.

choices.text: The student combined reflection, revision, and presentation skills to improve readiness
choices.feedback: Correct. The statement shows several Unit 6 skills working together.

choices.text: The student no longer needs feedback
choices.feedback: Feedback can still support continued growth.

questions.prompt: A strong closing statement in a portfolio presentation can summarize growth and readiness.

choices.text: False
choices.feedback: A closing statement can help summarize the main message of a portfolio presentation.

choices.text: True
choices.feedback: Correct. A strong closing can connect the portfolio evidence to growth and readiness.

questions.prompt: A student has strong portfolio artifacts but presents them in random order with little explanation. What is the biggest problem?

choices.text: The presentation may be harder for the audience to understand
choices.feedback: This is a real problem, but the correct answer in this set explains both organization and explanation together.

choices.text: The student has too much evidence
choices.feedback: The issue described is the lack of clear organization and explanation, not simply the amount of evidence.

choices.text: The presentation lacks clear structure and explanation, which weakens understanding of the artifacts
choices.feedback: Correct. Strong artifacts still need clear organization and explanation during presentation.

choices.text: The audience should organize the presentation themselves
choices.feedback: The presenter is responsible for organizing and explaining the content.

questions.prompt: Before a final portfolio presentation, which step would best improve the student's readiness?

choices.text: Reviewing key artifacts, revising weak areas, and practicing explanations
choices.feedback: This is a strong preparation step, but the correct answer in this set is the fuller version that emphasizes both reflection and presentation readiness.

choices.text: Skipping reflection and relying only on memory
choices.feedback: Reflection can help a student prepare more effectively.

choices.text: Presenting without choosing the strongest evidence
choices.feedback: A strong presentation should focus on selected evidence.

choices.text: Reviewing growth, revising key items, and practicing how to present the strongest evidence
choices.feedback: Correct. This combines the main preparation steps for Unit 6.

questions.prompt: Why does Unit 6 focus on both presentation and reflection?

choices.text: Because students should only talk about artifacts without evaluating growth
choices.feedback: Unit 6 is meant to include both explanation of evidence and reflection on growth.

choices.text: Because reflection removes the need for revision
choices.feedback: Reflection, revision, and presentation all support one another.

choices.text: Because presentation matters more than the quality of the portfolio
choices.feedback: Presentation matters, but strong evidence and reflection also matter.

choices.text: Because students must be able to explain their work and evaluate how they have developed
choices.feedback: Correct. Unit 6 combines communication and self-evaluation to show professional growth.
